sections.markdown:
# Reader Macros

Macros transform parsed code. **Reader macros** transform *characters* into parsed code — they extend the syntax of Lisp itself.

Every character the reader sees has an entry in the current **readtable**. Most characters are `constituent` (they form tokens). A few are special:

* `(` calls the reader function that builds lists.
* `'` calls the one that expands to `(quote ...)`.
* `"` starts a string.
* `;` starts a line comment.
* `#` is a **dispatch** character — it reads the next character and looks that up in a sub-table. That's how you get `#'foo` (function), `#(1 2 3)` (vector literal), `#\A` (character), `#P"/tmp"` (pathname), `#X` / `#B` / `#O` (radix), `#|...|#` (block comment), `#.form` (read-time eval), and so on.

Two APIs define your own:

* `(set-macro-character char fn)` — when the reader sees `char`, it calls `fn` with the stream and the character.
* `(set-dispatch-macro-character disp sub fn)` — when it sees `disp` followed by `sub`, it calls `fn`.

Your function reads whatever characters it wants from the stream and returns a Lisp object that takes the place of the raw characters.

**Warning:** reader macros are global mutable state. Install them on a *copy* of the readtable and use `let` + `*readtable*` to scope them, or you'll mangle code elsewhere in your image.

The example below installs a tiny `#?` dispatch macro that reads one form and wraps it in a list of `:maybe`.

sections.explain: The dispatch #' expands to (FUNCTION FOO); the car of that list is the symbol FUNCTION.
#( is another built-in dispatch: it reads a vector literal. The printed form round-trips.
#\ reads the next token as a character name. (The source string doubles the backslash for JSON.)

sections.code: (let ((*readtable* (copy-readtable))) (set-dispatch-macro-character #\# #\? (lambda (stream char1 char2) (declare (ignore char1 char2)) (list :maybe (read stream t nil t)))) (read-from-string "#?42"))
sections.explain: We installed a local #? reader that wraps the next form. The whole thing is scoped by our LET on *READTABLE*.

sections.prompt: Write INSTALL-BANG that clobbers the current *readtable* to make #! a dispatch reader macro that reads one form and returns (negate FORM). Then provide READ-BANG which runs INSTALL-BANG on a LOCAL copy of the readtable and reads STRING, returning the resulting object. (Using a local readtable keeps the change from leaking.)
sections.hint: install-bang: (set-dispatch-macro-character #\# #\! (lambda (stream c1 c2) (declare (ignore c1 c2)) (list 'negate (read stream t nil t))) rt). read-bang: (let ((*readtable* (copy-readtable))) (install-bang *readtable*) (read-from-string string)).
